title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa: Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. They will engage with local pūrākau (stories) of Ngāti Kahungunu ancestors who protected these waters, and examine contemporary challenges facing the stream including urban runoff and biodiversity loss.
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret oral histories and written texts about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to identify key themes of kaitiakitanga
Create a persuasive written piece (800-1000 words) advocating for the protection of the stream, incorporating both mātauranga Māori and scientific evidence
Collaborate effectively in a small group to plan and deliver a 5-minute oral presentation proposing a specific kaitiaki action for the stream
Use appropriate te reo Māori vocabulary and concepts (e.g., mauri, whakapapa, manaaki) when discussing environmental guardianship

activities.title: Whakapapa of the Waters
activities.description: Students begin by visiting Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, making observational sketches and recordings. Back in class, they analyze traditional pūrākau about the stream shared by a local kaumatua, identifying literary devices and cultural values expressed.

activities.title: Voices of the Kaitiaki
activities.description: Students interview representatives from local environmental groups (e.g., Friends of Mangakōtukutuku Stream) and Ngāti Kahungunu iwi representatives. They transcribe key quotes and analyze different perspectives on stream protection.

activities.title: Data and Narrative Weaving
activities.description: Using water quality data from Hawke's Bay Regional Council, students create visual representations of environmental challenges. They then craft narratives that blend this scientific data with cultural perspectives learned from community voices.

activities.title: Persuasive Writing Workshop
activities.description: Through a series of writing workshops, students develop persuasive texts advocating for specific protection measures for the stream, incorporating evidence from both scientific and cultural sources.

activities.title: Kaitiaki Action Presentations
activities.description: Students work in small groups to develop and present practical action plans for stream protection, receiving feedback from community experts and peers.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu iwi environmental management plans from their website
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality monitoring data for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Local kaumatua Hōri Reti (expert in Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau)
The book 'Te Mātauranga o te Taiao' by Dr. Dan Hikuroa

tasks: Persuasive essay (800-1000 words) advocating for a specific protection strategy for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Group oral presentation proposing a practical kaitiaki action project
Learning journal documenting interviews, stream observations, and reflective responses

nzcAlignment: English: Processes and Strategies - Integrating sources of information, processes, and strategies to identify, form, and express ideas
English: Ideas - Show understanding of ideas within, across, and beyond texts
English: Language Features - Use a range of language features appropriately for a variety of effects
Social Sciences: Understand how people manage resources and respond to challenges
